region_of_origin: India (North Indian cuisine), served in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Prawn Kali Mirch. A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent node.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Prawn Kali Mirch is approximately 250 g, comprising 8–12 medium prawns in a black pepper and tomato-onion gravy, as served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn curry / cooked prawn preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Prawn Kali Mirch in ICMR-NIN tables. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Prawn curry' (cooked prawn in spiced tomato-onion gravy) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and USDA entry for tomato-onion-based curry sauce. Macros reflect a composite of cooked prawn (~55% by weight) and spiced gravy (~45% by weight) at 100 g edible portion. Sodium elevated to reflect restaurant-level seasoning. vitamin_d_mcg is not reported in ICMR-NIN prawn curry tables or USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175180 for this composite preparation; value set to -1 to indicate not reported by source rather than confirmed zero.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shrimp / prawn curry analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Kali Mirch. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Cooked prawns are a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food; the tomato-onion gravy contributes minimal digestible starch. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 10.5) / 100 = 2.6, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving ≈ 10.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from restaurant-level seasoning and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a 250 g restaurant serving may deliver approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 50–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~25)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Kali Mirch is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g) and low GI (~25), resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 2.6 per standard serving; however, sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol from prawns and high sodium from restaurant preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally high in dietary cholesterol (~135 mg per 100 g) but low in saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion frequency and choose preparations with minimal added oil and salt to manage overall cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Kali Mirch (also known as Jhinga Kali Mirch or Black Pepper Prawn Curry) is a North Indian seafood dish featuring whole or halved prawns cooked in a bold, aromatic black pepper-forward gravy made with tomatoes, onions, garlic, ginger, and a blend of Indian spices. 'Kali Mirch' is the Hindi term for black pepper, which is the defining flavour of this dish — delivering a sharp, pungent heat that distinguishes it from chilli-based curries. The dish is typically prepared by sautéing onions and tomatoes in oil, building a masala base, and simmering prawns until just cooked through to preserve their tender, slightly springy texture. The resulting dish has a deep reddish-brown gravy with a pronounced peppery aroma and a savoury, mildly tangy flavour profile. In Singapore, Prawn Kali Mirch is served at North Indian restaurants as a chef recommendation or signature dish, typically priced between SGD 19.90 and SGD 24.90 per portion. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish (~13.5 g protein, ~4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with a low glycemic index (~25), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated in restaurant preparations. Prawns provide selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine, supporting thyroid and neurological function.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Prawn Kali Mirch exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN prawn curry data and USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp values, with composite estimation for the gravy component. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood curry entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Prawn curry' and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180); no exact Prawn Kali Mirch entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium value (480 mg/100 g) reflects restaurant-level seasoning typical of Singapore North Indian restaurants and may vary significantly by establishment. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish has been published. 4. The dish is labelled 'Chef Recommendation' on field survey data menus — this is a menu marketing label and has been removed from the canonical food_name. 5. Halal status is assumed based on the dish composition (no pork, no alcohol); individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the cooked prawn and vegetable oil components used in this preparation. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is reported as -1.0: this nutrient is not reported in the ICMR-NIN prawn curry tables or in USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175180 for the composite food as adapted; -1 is used per G3 convention to indicate 'not reported by source' rather than a confirmed zero or estimated value. Estimation of vitamin D for this composite dish would be highly speculative and is therefore not attempted.
